title: Comment from Boone, Keith, HHS-ONC-2025-0005, HHS-ONC-2025-0005-0001, 2025-23896
comment_text:
As with all of my comments, please note that these are my personal opinions, and do not necessarily represent those of my employer (Audacioous Inquiry), or its parent company, PointClickCare.

I appreciate the care with which ASTP/ONC made in putting together this rule and the preceding withdrawal of the remaining HTI2 proposals, if the timing was a bit challenging as usual, dropping just before the holidays.

I have very few concerns about what was removed, although I am aware that many on the patient advocacy front have deeper concerns.  I think a better job could have been done in prefatory content explaining the rational for removals, and better highlighting that removal of some of these criteria represents "mission accomplished", and the expectation that having delivered these features which are widely used in certified product (or removing features that few actually attest to), the results would have less impact than would be expected based on a pure regulatory requirement.

My general sense is that the removed criteria either represent a capability that is unlikely to disappear from the market due to wide adoption, or was never really adopted in the first place.

My largest complaint is about the cost estimate on reviewers in #HTI5:

ONC says: "We estimate those costs to be $284,132 in total for an estimated 644 reviewers"

There are about 500 vendors with certified products.  More than half will review this rule.  In addition to developers and associations, consumers of Health IT will also review the rule (Health Systems and Hospitals).  

Arguably there are at least 500 organizations reviewing the content of HTI5. Each of those organizations will have 2-3 people on the review.  #HTI5 is 65 pages long.  I'm going to say it took me 2 hours to review it, which is at least what a pro focused on it would spend.  Associations will have a tiger team of 5-8 people on it.  Review teams will meet for 30 minutes to plan their review. Then they will review #HTI5 for at least an hour, maybe two. Then they will meet again to discuss their results (another 30 minutes). Then someone gets to write a response (at least another 30 minutes, more like 60).

That's 3 hours, times 3 people, times 500 organizations or 4500 hours. The reviewers are executives mostly, or senior staffers. The balanced cost about $150/hr. That works out to $675K, not just under $300K. Using my simple figures, a generous underestimate, #HTI5's estimate of reviewer cost is LOW.

The savings impact of #HTI5 is likely overestimated.  ONC estimates a savings of $174,843,873 in the first year. However, I don't see that happening. The cost to certify product is NOT going to go down much. It's not like you get to throw away popular delivered features.  At least half the effort in certification is planning.  That effort will be reduced by maybe 20%.  The number of tests you certify to will go down significantly, so maybe you spend half the time certifying after #HTI5 as you did previously.

Assuming a vendor saves 100 hours in recertification under #HTI5 (team of 5 times 20 hours), and pays 5000 less for certification, you get a savings for recertifications of new software version (the bulk of the ongoing costs) of about 15K per product, times 717, or ~$11M a year. That's significantly less than $174M.  Where did ONC go wrong on these estimates in #HTI5?  I think they went wrong by assuming the savings was in development costs.  But the number of new product certifications requiring development of removed requirements is very low.

Most certifications are effectively "recertifications" of products which already had these features.  That's maintenance of the feature, rather than development and integration.  The latter cost was incurred the first time the feature was implemented and does not reoccur. 

HL7 had having multiple workgroups review #HTI5.  Patient empowerment just spent a whole quarter (1.5 hours) with 10 people reviewing it.  

I figure the cost on that meeting alone reviewing #HTI5 is about $3K, and that at least six other workgroups or committees (e.g. the PAC) will review.  Then these will undergo review by the PAC. That's 6% of ONC's estimated 284K for review of the regulation my $3K is probably an UNDERESTIMATE of cost.

I understand EHRA spent a similar amount of time on its review across multiple workgroups, and that professional societies are also spending a good bit of time on it.  I know of organization investing in many meetings with senior leaders over the course of 6 weeks.
